verses.text: 10:1 O genuíno luto de Esdras em resposta ao pecado de seu povo levou muitos deles a se unirem a ele.
10:2 Secanias foi a primeira pessoa corajosa o suficiente para publicamente admitir que havia sido infiel a Deus. A admissão de culpa dá a esperança de perdão pelo pecado.

10:3 Uma aliança é um acordo vinculativo, neste caso especificamente para se divorciar . . . das esposas pagãs que o povo de Israel havia impropriamente desposado. Ao tomar esta ação, eles renovariam seu compromisso com a aliança do Sinai. • As mulheres geralmente recebiam a custódia de seus filhos quando um casamento falhava (cp. Hagar e Ismael, Gn 21.14).

10:4 é seu dever: Como especialista na lei (ver 7.10), Esdras era responsável por conduzir o povo a uma solução honrosa para Deus para o problema do casamento misto. Visto que a lei de Moisés não continha diretrizes explícitas para divorciar-se de esposas pagãs, Esdras precisava desenvolver um plano consistente com as exigências das Escrituras e justo para os participantes.
10:5 O juramento solene envolvia tanto uma promessa de tomar uma ação quanto uma maldição autoimposta por falhar em fazer o que foi prometido (ver Rt 1.16-17; 1Sm 14.24-28).

10:6 Johanan the son of Eliashib - Eliashib was high priest, and was succeeded in that office by his son Joiada, Neh 12:10. Probably Johanan here is the same as Jonathan in Nehemiah, who was the son of Joiada, and grandson of Eliashib. Some suppose that Johanan and Joiada were two names for the same person.

10:7 And they made proclamation,.... By the voice of an herald: throughout Judah and Jerusalem, unto all the children of the captivity; who were returned from it: that they should gather themselves together unto Jerusalem; within a time after mentioned.

10:8 se os líderes e anciãos assim decidissem: Era razoável para Esdras acrescentar esta cláusula de exceção, porque pode ser simplesmente impossível para algumas pessoas virem a Jerusalém para participar da resolução desse assunto.
10:9 em 19 de dezembro . . . estava chovendo: O tempo frio e úmido tornou a reunião ao ar livre desconfortável.

10:10 Ezra the priest stood up, and said—Having fully represented the enormity of their sin and urged them to dissolve their unlawful connections, he was gratified by receiving a prompt acknowledgment of the justice of his reproof and a promise of compliance with his recommendation. But as the weather was ungenial and the defaulters were too numerous to be passed in review at one time, it was resolved that a commission should be appointed to examine into the whole matter. These commissioners, assisted by the judges and elders of the respective cities, made a minute investigation into every case, and after three months' labor completely removed all traces of the abuse.

10:11 Now therefore make confession to the Lord God of your fathers,.... Of their sin, and express their detestation of it, and repentance for it: and do his pleasure; obey his will, and particularly in this case: and separate yourselves from the people of the land; the Canaanites, &c. have no fellowship with them, make no covenants, contracts, and alliances with them for the future: and from the strange wives; they had taken; put them away.

10:12 As thou hast said, so must we do - They all resolved to do what Ezra then commanded, they did put away their wives, even those by whom they had children; Ezr 10:44 : this was a great hardship on the women and children. Though by the Jewish laws such marriages were null and void, yet as the women they had taken did not know these laws, their case was deplorable. However, we may take it for granted that each of them received a portion according to the circumstances of their husbands, and that they and their children were not turned away desolate, but had such a provision as their necessities required.

10:13 But the people are many,.... Who have been guilty of this evil: and it is a time of much rain, and we are not able to stand without: in the street, because of the rain: neither is this a work of one day or two; to inquire into this affair, who they are that have taken strange wives, and to persuade or oblige them to put them away: for we are many that have transgressed in this thing; in marrying strange wives.

10:14 Let now our rulers of all the congregations stand,.... Let the great sanhedrim, or court of judicature at Jerusalem, be fixed and continued, and others: and let all them which have taken strange wives in our cities come at appointed times; to the court at Jerusalem, at certain and fixed known times of their sittings there for this purpose: and with the elders of every city and the judges thereof; the principal magistrates of it, who were to testify that upon search and inquiry those were the men and all the men in their city, that had taken strange wives, and that they had put them away according to the order of the princes and elders;

10:15 Não está claro por que essas quatro pessoas se opuseram ao plano. Talvez quisessem uma penalidade mais rigorosa, ou talvez elas ou os membros de suas famílias não quisessem se divorciar de suas esposas estrangeiras. Que houvesse apenas quatro dissidentes mostra o apoio esmagador que a política havia ganhado entre os demais exilados. Tristemente, alguns anos depois, um problema semelhante de casamento misto com esposas pagãs criou outra crise dentro da comunidade (Ne 9–10).

10:16 And the children of the captivity did so,.... Put away their strange wives: and Ezra the priest, with certain chief of the fathers, after the house of their fathers, and all of them by their names, were separated; from all their other business, and gave themselves up to attend to this wholly: and sat down in the first day of the tenth month; the month Tebeth, which answers to part of December and January; this was ten days after the assembly of all the people met and broke up: these took their places in the great court, and there sat to examine the matter;

10:17 And they made an end with all the men that had taken strange wives,.... Finished their inquiry and examination of them: by the first day of the first month; the month Nisan, answering to part of March and April; so that they were three months about this work.

10:18 THOSE THAT HAD TAKEN STRANGE WIVES. (Ezra 10:18-44) among the sons of the priests—From the names of so many men of rank appearing in the following list, some idea may be formed of the great and complicated difficulties attending the reformatory work.

10:19 they gave their hands—that is, came under a solemn engagement, which was usually ratified by pledging the right hand (Pro 6:1; Ezek 17:18). The delinquents of the priestly order bound themselves to do like the common Israelites (Ezr 10:25), and sought to expiate their sin by sacrificing a ram as a trespass offering. Next: Nehemiah Introduction

10:20 Of the sons of Immer; Hanani, and Zebadiah: The roll of priests who married foreign wives opens with the house of Immer. That the priesthood itself stood among the offenders intensifies the crisis — Ezra's reform spared no caste (cp. Ezra 9:1-2; Neh 13:28). Restoration required confession from the leaders before the people could follow.

10:21 Of the sons of Harim; Maaseiah, and Elijah, and Shemaiah, and Jehiel, and Uzziah: A second priestly house enrolled among those who put away foreign wives. Harim is also listed at the return (Ezra 2:39) and among signatories of the covenant renewal (Neh 10:5). Repentance, not lineage, restored their priestly standing.

10:22 Of the sons of Pashur: A third priestly clan (Ezra 2:38) named in the reform. Pashur's descendants would later sign Nehemiah's covenant (Neh 10:3), suggesting that the discipline of Ezra 10 did not break the priesthood but purified it. Repentance preserved continuity of office.

10:23 Of Levites, only six names are given, and that without stated the houses to which they belonged. From Ezr 2:40, however, it appears that they were of the sons of Jeshua and Kadmiel there mentioned. "Kelaiah, the same is Kelita;" the latter is the usual name of the person in question, and that which he bears in Neh 8:7 and Neh 10:11. Jozabad also reappears in Neh 8:7.
10:24 Of singers one, and of porters three names are given; comp. Ezr 2:41-42. In all, ten Levites.
10:25 Moreover of Israel - That is, as Calmet observes, simple Israelites, to distinguish them from the priests, Levites, and singers, mentioned in Ezr 10:18, Ezr 10:23, Ezr 10:24.

10:26 Of the sons of Elam: The list shifts from priests (10:18-22) to lay Israelites; Elam was among the larger returnee families (Ezra 2:7). The transition shows that the reform reached every stratum — the same standard of covenant fidelity applied to priest and layman alike.

10:27 Of the sons of Zattu: Another large lay family (Ezra 2:8) named among those who repented of mixed marriage. The repetition of family-name + sons-by-name preserves accountability — neither anonymous penance nor blanket absolution, but each man named before the community.

10:28 Of the sons also of Bebai: A smaller clan (Ezra 2:11; eight names total). The thoroughness of the list — sometimes only four or five men per family — testifies both to the limited reach of the offense and to the precision of the reform.

10:29 Of the sons of Bani: Six men from one of the substantial returnee houses (Ezra 2:10). The Bani clan reappears repeatedly in the list (10:34, 38), suggesting the family had spread out and intermarried widely.

10:30 Of the sons of Pahath-moab: The largest single-family entry in the chapter (eight men), matching Pahath-moab's prominence at the return (Ezra 2:6 — 2,812 souls). Larger families faced proportional accountability; size offered no shelter.

10:31 Of the sons of Harim: A lay branch of Harim, distinct from the priestly Harim of 10:21. The doubled occurrence within a single chapter shows how widespread foreign marriage had become — touching both pulpit and pew of the post-exilic community.

10:32 Benjamin, Malluch, and Shemariah: The continuation of the Harim list (10:31). Splitting the entry across verses preserves the original document's pacing; the Chronicler-style register is faithfully reproduced from Ezra's own scribal records.

10:33 Of the sons of Hashum: Hashum's family appears in earlier registers (Ezra 2:19) and at the covenant renewal (Neh 10:18). Seven men named here suggest the family bore notable involvement in the offense — and notable willingness to remedy it.

10:34 Of the sons of Bani: A second listing of Bani's offenders (cp. 10:29). The Bani name spans more than ten verses in the register (10:34-42), pointing either to multiple branches sharing a name or to one very large family widely tangled in mixed marriage.

10:35 Benaiah, Bedeiah, Chelluh: The Bani list continues. The names — many bearing *El* or *Yah* compounds (Benaiah, 'YHWH has built') — show that men of Yahwistic households had still drifted into prohibited unions. Pious upbringing did not immunize against compromise.

10:36 Vaniah, Meremoth, Eliashib: A name like Eliashib (compare the high priest of Neh 3:1) appears here as an offender, again confirming that the reform reached deep into prominent households. Common names complicate identification, but the principle stands: status did not exempt.

10:37 Mattaniah, Mattenai, and Jaasau: The Bani list continues without commentary. The bare register-style mirrors the Babylonian and Persian administrative documents Ezra would have known, lending the reform record official weight.

10:38 And Bani, and Binnui, Shimei: A man named Bani appears within the Bani list itself — a son named after the patriarch, common in extended Israelite families. The register's literal repetition resists tidy reorganization, preserving each man's name.

10:39 And Shelemiah, and Nathan, and Adaiah: Three more names continue the Bani roll. The reform was costly: each man named here renounced wife and (often) children (cp. 10:3, 44). The pain of these severings makes the bare list of names a record of grief as well as obedience.

10:40 Machnadebai, Shashai, Sharai: Names of uncertain meaning — Machnadebai may be Persian in origin, hinting that exile-era families had absorbed foreign cultural markers even in their naming practices. The reform sought to recover covenant identity at every level.

10:41 Azareel, and Shelemiah, Shemariah: The list nears completion. Yahwistic names dominate (Azareel — 'God has helped'; Shemariah — 'YHWH has guarded'), reminding the reader that the offenders were not pagan but covenant Israelites who had departed from the law (Deut 7:3).

10:42 Shallum, Amariah, and Joseph: The Bani register closes. The last name Joseph echoes the patriarch and recalls Israel's long history of preserved identity in foreign lands — a memory the reform now reactivates for the post-exilic generation.

10:43 Of the sons of Nebo: The final family in the chapter — Nebo, a smaller clan (Ezra 2:29; Neh 7:33). The verse concludes the personnel roster; verse 44 supplies the brief summary of the wives and children put away, closing the book on a costly act of obedience.
10:44 e alguns até tinham filhos com essas esposas: Os casais que tinham filhos provavelmente haviam se casado antes de Esdras voltar a Jerusalém; mesmo eles não foram excluídos do decreto.
